learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells: Beyond Silicon Limits
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells offer higher efficiency and lower costs than traditional silicon cells, potentially revolutionizing solar energy adoption. Their lightweight, flexible design enables innovative applications like building-integrated photovoltaics, accelerating the transition to renewable energy.
learn.c1_text: Perovskite cells achieve lab efficiencies over 25%, rivaling silicon. Their unique crystal structure allows better light absorption, with some prototypes reaching 33.7% efficiency in tandem configurations. This outperforms standard silicon cells (15-22%) and reduces land use needs.
learn.c2_text: Production costs could drop to $0.20-$0.30 per watt by 2030, compared to silicon's $0.50-$0.70. Solution-based manufacturing (like inkjet printing) enables roll-to-roll production, making perovskites ideal for large-scale, low-cost solar farms. Example: India's Rewa Solar Park uses silicon, but perovskites could slash future costs.
learn.c3_text: Perovskites degrade faster than silicon under heat/humidity. India's tropical climate poses challenges, but research at IIT Bombay focuses on encapsulation techniques to extend lifespan. Policy support via MNRE's R&D grants (₹150 Cr allocated 2023) aims to commercialize stable variants by 2027.

learn.title: Iron-Air Batteries: Long-Duration Storage
learn.intro: Iron-air batteries store energy for 100+ hours using abundant materials, offering a low-cost alternative to lithium-ion. They excel in stabilizing grids with intermittent renewables, crucial for India's renewable energy goals.
learn.c1_text: Iron is 1,000x cheaper than lithium, with India having 4.2% global reserves. The battery uses iron anode and oxygen cathode, eliminating scarce materials. Pilot plants in the US (e.g., Form Energy) aim for $10/kWh, 50x cheaper than lithium-ion.
learn.c2_text: They endure 100,000+ cycles vs. 5,000 for lithium-ion. A 100-hour discharge cycle suits India's monsoon-dependent solar/wind grid. For example, Tamil Nadu's hybrid plants could use iron-air for 100+ hour storage, reducing reliance on diesel backups.
learn.c3_text: India needs 270 GW storage by 2030 but currently has 2.5 GW. Iron-air aligns with PM Gati Shakti's infrastructure goals. Policy: FAME II encourages domestic battery manufacturing, with iron-air fitting into India's 2030 target of 500 GW renewables.
learn.stat4: FAME II target: 50% domestic battery components by 2025

learn.title: Geothermal Energy: Earth's Heat Powerhouse
learn.intro: Geothermal taps Earth's internal heat for baseload power and heating, providing reliable clean energy. India's geothermal potential in regions like Rajasthan and Himachal Pradesh could diversify its renewable mix.
learn.c1_text: Geothermal plants operate 24/7, unlike solar/wind. The 200 MW Puga plant in Ladakh (India) uses dry steam, achieving 90% capacity factor vs. solar's 25%. This ensures stable grid supply even during cloudy days.
learn.c2_text: Geothermal heat pumps provide heating/cooling with 500% efficiency. India's 2022 National Geothermal Mission aims to map 10,000 MW potential. Example: A 20 MW plant in Rajasthan could supply heating for 20,000 homes annually.
learn.c3_text: India lacks specific geothermal policies. The 2022 mission targets 1,000 MW by 2030, but only 0.1% of potential is tapped. Iceland meets 40% of energy needs via geothermal; India could replicate this with targeted investments.

learn.title: Green Hydrogen Production: PEM vs Alkaline
learn.intro: Green hydrogen, produced via water electrolysis using renewables, is key for decarbonizing industries. PEM and alkaline electrolyzers differ in efficiency and scalability, impacting India's hydrogen mission.
learn.c1_text: PEM electrolyzers convert 60-70% of electricity to hydrogen, vs. 50-60% for alkaline. They operate at lower temperatures (70-80°C), ideal for variable renewable sources like solar. Example: Germany's ITM Power uses PEM for 90%+ efficiency.
learn.c2_text: Alkaline systems are cheaper ($300-$500/kW vs. PEM's $1,000-$1,500/kW) and suited for large-scale, steady production. India's National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 GW by 2030, favoring alkaline for bulk storage. Gujarat's Deso gift plant uses alkaline tech.
learn.c3_text: India aims for 5 GW green hydrogen by 2030, creating 100,000 jobs. Policy: The 2022 mission offers $2/kg subsidy for PEM and $1.5/kg for alkaline. Challenges: Limited domestic electrolyzer manufacturing and skilled workforce.

learn.title: Hydrogen Colors: Blue, Green, Grey
learn.intro: Hydrogen's color code clarifies its production method. Green (renewable), blue (fossil with CCS), and grey (fossil without CCS) hydrogen have varying environmental impacts, shaping global and Indian energy strategies.
learn.c1_text: Green hydrogen uses solar/wind-powered electrolysis, emitting zero CO₂. It's critical for India's net-zero goal, with projects like Rajasthan's 1 GW plant targeting 2030. However, it requires cheap renewables and storage.
learn.c2_text: Blue hydrogen uses natural gas with carbon capture (CCS), cutting 90% emissions. But CCS is unproven at scale. India's NTPC plans a 1 GW blue plant in Gujarat, but experts argue it delays green transition.
learn.c3_text: Grey hydrogen (95% global production) emits 20 kg CO₂/kg H₂. India produces 6.5 MMTPA grey hydrogen (2023), mostly for fertilizers. Policy: The 2022 mission bans grey hydrogen imports, pushing for domestic green production.

learn.title: Capacity Factor Demystified: Real vs Rated Power
learn.intro: Capacity factor measures how much energy a plant generates vs. its maximum output. Understanding this metric is vital for grid planning, as solar/wind's variability affects reliability.
learn.c1_text: A 1 MW solar plant with 25% CF generates 262,800 kWh/year (365 days × 24 hrs × 0.25). India's Pavagada Solar Park (600 MW) has ~28% CF, highlighting solar's intermittency. This necessitates storage or hybrid systems.
learn.c2_text: Onshore wind has 30-40% CF, offshore 45-50%. India's Ennore Wind Park (40 MW) averages 35% CF. Lower CF means wind requires complementary sources like solar to ensure grid stability.
learn.c3_text: India's avg solar CF is 22%, wind 32%. The 2024 grid code mandates 25% minimum CF for new solar bids. Example: Tamil Nadu's hybrid plants combine solar (25% CF) and wind (35% CF) to balance output.
learn.stat4: Grid code: 25% min CF for solar (2024)
